definition: A maturity-dependent correlation regime where 2Y-SPX correlation is negative (-0.15 to -0.30, reflecting Fed credibility) while 30Y-SPX correlation is positive (+0.05 to +0.15, reflecting fiscal supply dynamics). This bifurcation is a leading indicator of fiscal dominance. Falsifiable prediction: when 2Y-SPX correlation turns positive, the regime has fully shifted.
evidence: Observed in UK during Truss crisis (short gilts rallied, long gilts collapsed) and Italy 2011
Vol-targeting strategies ($500B-$1T AUM) mechanically reduce equity exposure when stock-bond correlations rise
Identical correlation ranges cited independently across 5 agents
Front end governed by Fed expectations, long end by fiscal supply/term premium
Two prior split-correlation episodes identified: 1965-69 and 2003-06
2003-06 resolved back to monetary dominance (negative correlation)
Long-duration Treasuries losing hedging property agreed across all agents
open_questions: What is the noise-to-signal ratio in real-time correlation tracking?
Does the bifurcation persist across different volatility regimes?
Is this bifurcation a permanent regime shift or temporary?
Sample period is short (~3 years of post-QE data) — is it statistically robust?
What is the magnitude of forced selling from vol-targeting strategies during fiscal stress?
Will the current split resolve toward fiscal dominance (1965-69 pattern) or back to monetary dominance (2003-06 pattern)?
Are there leading indicators that distinguish which resolution pathway is more likely?
N=2 for prior transitions — is this sufficient for inference?
